     AI-related: mios-selinux-init, mios-selinux-init.service, selinux-init, 37-selinux.sh, 19-k3s-selinux.sh, mios_kvmfr, mios_portabled -->
# SELinux on MiOS

> Mode: **enforcing**. Custom per-rule policy modules are *compiled and staged*
> into `/usr/share/selinux/packages/mios/` at build time but **not loaded** there
> — they are loaded on the booted host by `mios-selinux-init.service`
> (`/usr/libexec/mios/selinux-init`), gated by the sentinel
> `/var/lib/mios/.selinux-init-done`. Module compilation + the build-time
> `semanage` booleans/fcontexts live in `automation/37-selinux.sh`; the k3s policy
> is compiled in `automation/19-k3s-selinux.sh`.

## Purpose — why SELinux matters to MiOS as a whole

MiOS is a single immutable bootc/OCI Fedora image that is *also* a local,
self-replicating agentic AI OS. Both halves run on the same host: an agent stack
(agent-pipe `:8640`, MiOS-Hermes `:8642`, the inference lanes, pgvector `:5432`)
that can launch apps, run containers, and pass a GPU into a lane, sitting next to
a desktop the operator actually uses. SELinux enforcing is the mandatory-access
layer that keeps that powerful agentic surface confined: it is one of the three
zero-trust pillars (`fapolicyd` deny-by-default execution, SELinux enforcing, and
signed/measured boot) called out in `concepts/architecture.md`.

The wrinkle is that MiOS is immutable. `/usr` is a composefs-verity-sealed tree,
so any SELinux state baked at image-build time would be frozen against the
*build container's* policy store, not the host's running policy. That is the
reasoning behind MiOS's split model — **compile and stage in the image, load on
the host** — described below. It is the SELinux analogue of how CDI specs are
regenerated per boot rather than baked: the immutable image ships the *artifacts*,
and a one-shot first-boot service reconciles them with the live system.

## Project

- SELinux: <https://github.com/SELinuxProject/selinux>
- Reference policy: <https://github.com/SELinuxProject/refpolicy>
- Fedora SELinux user-space: <https://github.com/fedora-selinux>

## Custom policy modules

The modules are not vendored as standalone `.te` files. `automation/37-selinux.sh`
defines each one inline (a `MIOS_POLICIES[<name>]` here-doc), compiles it with
`checkmodule` + `semodule_package`, and installs the resulting `mios_<name>.pp`
into `/usr/share/selinux/packages/mios/`. A module whose required type is absent
in the build-container policy is **skipped, not fatal** (best-effort staging).
`automation/19-k3s-selinux.sh` separately clones, compiles, and stages the
upstream `k3s.pp` into the same directory.

These are narrow, per-denial allow rules for known Fedora Rawhide / systemd-260
behaviours and for MiOS's own hardware/agent paths. The set of interest to the
rest of the doc tree:

| Module | Purpose |
| --- | --- |
| `mios_kvmfr` | Lets the confined VM domain (`svirt_t`) open/read/write/map the Looking Glass KVMFR shared-memory `chr_file` (`device_t`) — VFIO-passthrough display without going permissive |
| `mios_portabled` | `init_t` ↔ `systemd_portabled_t` D-Bus `send_msg` for sysext/confext (systemd-portabled) activation |
| `mios_coreos_bootmount` | `coreos_boot_mount_generator_t` write access to generated unit files (bootc/CoreOS boot mount path) |
| `mios_bootupd` / `mios_bootupd_state` | `bootupd_t` read/lock access to `boot_t` files (bootloader update under bootc) |
| `mios_homed_varhome` | `systemd_homed_t` search/read of `home_root_t` (the `/var/home` home layout) |
| `mios_fapolicyd*`, `mios_accountsd*`, `mios_resolved*`, `mios_chcon*`, `mios_gdm*` | Desktop/daemon denials (fapolicyd↔GDM/GNOME-Remote-Desktop sockets, accountsservice symlink/D-Bus reads, resolved socket writes, GDM cache writes) |
| `k3s.pp` | Upstream k3s SELinux policy (from `automation/19-k3s-selinux.sh`) for the one-node k3s+Ceph cluster path |

> Historical note: an earlier revision of this doc described exactly "five custom
> modules" (`mios_portabled`, `mios_kvmfr`, `mios_cdi`, `mios_quadlet`,
> `mios_sysext`). That list is stale — the current `37-selinux.sh` generates the
> broader set above (and CDI/Quadlet/sysext confinement is handled via the
> upstream `container_t`/`svirt_t` domains plus the booleans below rather than
> dedicated `mios_cdi`/`mios_quadlet`/`mios_sysext` modules). Always treat
> `automation/37-selinux.sh` + `automation/19-k3s-selinux.sh` as the SSOT for the
> module list; `semodule -l | grep mios` enumerates what actually loaded on a host.

## Booleans

**Build-time (`semanage import` in `automation/37-selinux.sh`)** — applied
best-effort during the build, re-applied persistently at first boot:

- `container_use_cephfs=on` — let containers read/write CephFS
- `virt_use_samba=on` — let libvirt VMs talk to host Samba shares
- `virt_use_nfs=on`, `virt_sandbox_use_all_caps=on` — VM/sandbox plumbing
- `container_manage_cgroup=on` — Quadlet container cgroup management
- `domain_can_mmap_files=on`, `daemons_dump_core=on`, `nis_enabled=on`

**Runtime-persistent (`booleans.conf` next to the staged `.pp` modules)** —
`semanage` is typically inoperative inside an OCI build (no running policy), so
this manifest is applied on the host by `selinux-init` via `setsebool -P`:

- `container_use_devices=on` — **required** for the GPU container/CDI flow
  (NVIDIA, AMD ROCm, Intel xe). Without it the first GPU container start — i.e.
  the `mios-llm-light` inference lane requesting `nvidia.com/gpu=all` — is denied
  on an enforcing host.

## Fcontexts

Declared via the same build-time `semanage import`:

- `/boot/bootupd-state.json` → `boot_t`
- `/usr/share/accountsservice/interfaces(/.*)?` → `accountsd_var_lib_t`
- `/var/lib/ceph(/.*)?` → `ceph_var_lib_t`, `/var/log/ceph(/.*)?` → `ceph_log_t`
- `/var/lib/gnome-remote-desktop(/.*)?` → `xdm_var_lib_t`

A `restorecon -R /boot /etc /usr /var` runs first to repair labels across the
major trees.

## Why "compiled and staged, not loaded at build"

`/usr` is sealed by composefs verity. If `37-selinux.sh` ran `semodule -i` during
the build, the resulting policy state would be frozen against the build
container's store — and loading would anyway require `/sys/fs/selinux` mounted in
the build container. So the build only **stages** the compiled `.pp` artifacts
(which `bootc container lint`, Architectural Law 4, can validate as plain files
without a live policy load).

The actual load is deferred to the host:

- **`mios-selinux-init.service`** runs `/usr/libexec/mios/selinux-init` early
  (`After=local-fs.target`, `Before=sysinit.target`), guarded by
  `ConditionPathExists=!/var/lib/mios/.selinux-init-done` and
  `ConditionPathExists=/usr/share/selinux/packages/mios`.
- `selinux-init` detects the SELinux mode, then `semodule -i`'s every `*.pp` in
  `/usr/share/selinux/packages/mios/` that is not already loaded, and applies the
  persistent booleans from `booleans.conf` via `setsebool -P`.
- It then writes the sentinel `/var/lib/mios/.selinux-init-done` so it fires only
  once per deploy. After a `bootc upgrade` ships new/changed modules, the new
  image carries a fresh `/usr` (the sentinel lives in `/var`, persisting across
  upgrades) — re-runs are operator-driven if the policy set changes.

This load-on-host design keeps `bootc upgrade` from creating opaque SELinux
policy layers, exactly as `automation/19-k3s-selinux.sh` notes for the k3s policy:
ship the compiled artifact in immutable `/usr`, never `semodule -i` at build.

## Verification

```bash
getenforce                          # must print "Enforcing"
ausearch -m AVC -ts recent          # any recent denials
semodule -l | grep mios             # list the loaded mios_* modules
semodule -l | grep -x k3s           # the staged k3s policy
ls /usr/share/selinux/packages/mios # staged .pp artifacts + booleans.conf
journalctl -u mios-selinux-init     # what the first-boot loader did
getsebool container_use_devices     # must be on (GPU/CDI flow)
```
